statement.markdown: A peak element in an array is an element that is not smaller than its neighbors. Given an array of integers, find a peak element and return its index. If the input array is empty, return -1. You may assume that the input array always has a peak element. The array may contain duplicate elements. Note that a peak element does not have to be strictly greater than its neighbors, it can be equal to them. For example, in the array [1, 2, 3, 1], 3 is a peak element because it is not smaller than its neighbors. In the array [1, 2, 1, 3, 5, 6, 4], both 2 and 6 are peak elements because they are not smaller than their neighbors.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer n, the number of elements in the array. The second line contains n space-separated integers, the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: The index of a peak element in the array.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array

examples.explanation: The peak element is 3 at index 2
The peak element is 6 at index 5
The peak element is 5 at index 0
The peak element is 3 at index 1

hints.text: The problem asks for a peak element in the array, which is an element that is not smaller than its neighbors. Think about how you can use this definition to find a peak element.
Since the array has a peak element, you can start by looking at the edges of the array. The first element and the last element are potential peak elements because they only have one neighbor.

You can use a binary search approach to find the peak element. Start by finding the middle element of the array. If the middle element is greater than or equal to its neighbors, it is a peak element. Otherwise, you can discard the half of the array that does not contain the peak element.
To implement the binary search, you can use a recursive approach or an iterative approach. The key idea is to repeatedly divide the search space in half until you find the peak element.

The core technique to use here is binary search, which is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that finds the position of a target value within a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half.

editorial.title: Finding a Peak Element with Logarithmic Efficiency
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding an index in an array where the element is not smaller than its neighbors. A brute force approach would involve checking every element, but this is inefficient for large arrays. A more efficient approach involves leveraging the properties of peaks in arrays to reduce the search space significantly.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force method involves iterating through the array and checking each element to see if it's a peak by comparing it with its neighbors. This approach is straightforward but results in a time complexity of O(n), which can be slow for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution involves using a modified binary search algorithm. The observation that unlocks this approach is that if an element is not a peak, then moving towards the side where the neighboring element is larger will eventually lead to a peak. This is because the array must have at least one peak element.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a binary search approach. It starts by checking the middle element of the current search range. If the middle element is a peak, the algorithm returns its index. If not, it adjusts the search range to the half where a peak must exist based on the comparison with neighboring elements.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by observing that the algorithm always reduces the search space by half in each step until it finds a peak. The base cases (first, last, and single-element array) are handled correctly. The algorithm maintains the invariant that a peak exists within the current search range, ensuring termination and correctness.
editorial.dryRun: Testing the approach with an array like [1, 2, 3, 1] helps validate its correctness. Starting with the full range, the middle element (index 1, value 2) is not a peak, so the algorithm moves to the right half. The next middle element (index 2, value 3) is a peak, and the algorithm correctly returns index 2.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly handling edge cases such as arrays with one or two elements.
Failing to adjust the search range correctly based on comparisons.
Not properly checking for peak conditions at the array boundaries.
